metadata.format_notes: User messages are verbatim. Assistant responses are substantive summaries (actual responses were often long; full decisions captured in PROPOSAL.md, python-skill-script-style-requirements.md, and proposal-critique-v05-revision.md). Tool-use descriptions omitted; only conversational exchange captured. This pass was a bash-to-Python language migration, not a defect critique.

turns.user: Read /data/projects/livespec/brainstorming/approach-2-nlspec-based/python-conversion-prompt.md and follow
turns.assistant: Read the python-conversion-prompt.md (a Python-specialization of critique-interview-prompt.md). Resolved versioning knobs: N=005, NEXT=006, CRITIQUE=05. Loaded required context: PROPOSAL.md (v005), bash-skill-script-style-requirements.md, goals-and-non-goals.md, livespec-nlspec-spec.md, nlspec-spec.md, lifecycle diagrams, terminology summary, prior-art.md, subdomains-and-unsolved-routing.md, and v003/v004/v005 proposed_changes + revisions. Produced the v006 conversion plan at brainstorming/proposed_changes/v006-proposed-change-proposal-critique-v05.md with 21 proposals (P1-P21) labelled by axis (language-floor, dependency-strategy, tooling, layout, rule-simplification, enforcement-suite-shape, backwards-compatibility, architecture-pattern).

Asked P1 (Python version floor) with four options: 3.10, 3.11, 3.12, 3.8/3.9. Recommended 3.10 — matches 'modern Python' expectations, preinstalled on Ubuntu 22.04 LTS, modern typing/match syntax, no mise required on macOS for the floor.
turns.user: [P1 answered: 3.10 floor.]

turns.assistant: Asked P2 (runtime deps): stdlib-only + drop jq (recommended); vendored PyYAML+jsonschema; user-installed via pip; or keep jq + stdlib.
turns.user: How much complexity does a bespoke JSON validator entail?

turns.assistant: Provided honest estimate: ~300-500 LOC total for all schemas livespec needs. Used dataclass-based validation with type-hint introspection; conditional requirements add per-schema custom validators; enum-ish fields are one-liners; error-path helpers ~30 LOC. Trade-offs vs jsonschema (standardization, interop) and msgspec/pydantic (less boilerplate but compiled-wheel / Rust core concerns). Conclusion: bespoke is bounded-complexity, not a schema-engine-from-scratch.
turns.user: re ask

turns.assistant: Re-asked P2. Answered.
turns.user: [P2 answered: python3 only; stdlib; drop jq.]

turns.assistant: Asked P3 (dependency shipping mechanism) as a confirmation given P2(a).
turns.user: What are the actual implications of requiring dependencies like JSON schema and result? What does this mean for users at run time?

turns.assistant: Broke down user-visible impact across three strategies: (A) stdlib-only — one dep (python3), no install steps, no first-run friction; (B) vendored — identical UX as A for users, bundle grows ~300-500 KB, maintainer carries re-vendor work; (C) user-installed via pip — PEP 668 externally-managed-environment issues, venv activation, corp-network blocks, every version bump requires user pip re-run. Stated A and B are indistinguishable to users; C meaningfully worse. A-vs-B is purely an us-decision.
turns.user: Are there any licensing concerns with vendoring if I want to release this as MIT licensed?

turns.assistant: Walked through license compatibility: MIT compatible with MIT / BSD-2 / BSD-3 / Apache-2.0 vendoring; GPL-family incompatible (none of the candidates are GPL). Listed each candidate's license (jsonschema MIT, PyYAML MIT, returns BSD-2, result MIT, expression MIT, msgspec BSD-3, pydantic MIT). Flagged the transitive-dep gotcha: modern jsonschema depends on rpds-py (Rust backend, not pure-Python, not portable to vendor). Listed NOTICES.md + per-lib LICENSE preservation as the operational compliance steps. No licensing blocks any candidate.
turns.user: Is there a pure Python alternative to JSON schema that is popular and maintained?

turns.assistant: Identified fastjsonschema (horejsek/python-fastjsonschema): pure Python, MIT-licensed, zero transitive deps, ~10-20x faster than jsonschema (generates validator code from schema), used by Jupyter (nbformat) and cookiecutter. Partial Draft 2019-09/2020-12 but full Draft 7 support — sufficient for livespec's narrow needs. Added P3(a') vendored-fastjsonschema option. Noted the Result library wasn't yet addressed.
turns.user: You are just focusing on JSON schema. I also care about the result library.

turns.assistant: Covered the Result/ROP library landscape: returns (dry-python, BSD-2, ~3-4k LOC, full ROP ecosystem including IOResult/@safe/do-notation/pyright plugin), result (rustedpy, MIT, ~500 LOC, minimal Rust-style API), expression (MIT, F#-inspired ~2-3k LOC), hand-rolled (~50-150 LOC). All three libraries vendor cleanly. Hand-rolled is genuinely a clean win for just Result; for JSON Schema, standardized fastjsonschema gets publishable schemas. Suggested decoupling: P3 = pre-authorize vendoring as a mechanism, then P21 and JSON-validation pick on their own merits.
turns.user: OK, then I definitely want dry-python.  I want the strongest guardrails on the code possible, in terms of type safety and the loose coupling anbd high cohesion forced by ROP pattern.  This gives agentic implementors minimal room to do horrible things.

turns.assistant: Saved the guardrails principle as feedback memory (apply strongest reasonable defaults everywhere; prefer libraries that enforce patterns over hand-rolled equivalents). Locked P3 (vendoring authorized), P21 (vendored dry-python/returns for ROP), P6 (pyright strict non-negotiable).

Asked P4 (package layout): single livespec/ package (recommended) vs flat files vs src/ layout. Noted single-package enables mechanical enforcement of ROP/purity/private-call rules at package boundaries.
turns.user: [P4 answered: single package livespec/ with sub-packages.]

turns.assistant: Asked P5 (linter/formatter): ruff with strict rule selection (E F I B UP SIM C90 N RUF + PL + PTH) as recommended; alternatives of conservative rules, ruff+black, or legacy flake8 stack.
turns.user: [P5 answered: ruff with strict rule selection.]

turns.assistant: Asked P6 (type checker) as a formal confirmation — pyright strict gating locked in by guardrails principle.
turns.user: [P6 answered: pyright strict, gating.]

turns.assistant: Asked P7 (test framework): pytest + tmp_path + monkeypatch + parametrize + pytest-cov + pytest-icdiff recommended.
turns.user: 1, all the strictness.  Also, do we need to update the coverage and quality guidelines to 100% now that we have python?

turns.assistant: Confirmed P7 choice. Answered the coverage question by teeing up P8: kcov's DEBUG-trap artifact (the only reason for 95% pure-library tier in v005) disappears with coverage.py. Asked P8 with 100% line+branch everywhere + pragma escape as recommended option.
turns.user: [P8 answered: 100% line+branch everywhere, `# pragma: no cover — <reason>` as capped escape.]

turns.assistant: Asked P9 (complexity thresholds): Python-density-adjusted CCN ≤ 10, func ≤ 30 LLOC, file ≤ 200 LLOC, no args limit, max nesting ≤ 4, no waivers.
turns.user: [P9 answered: recommended thresholds, no waivers.]

turns.assistant: Asked P10 (enforcement suite shape) with a Makefile target list.
turns.user:
I'm confused.You seem to be conflating the makefile targets with what we actually run.  The only point of Makefile targets is to have some convenience for it to be executed via multiple mechanisms from CI, from Git pre- or post-hooks, or directly when necessary.   And for the first two former ones, it should definitely run everything.  Even if they are split into separate parts for clarity . For example, separate parts of a left hook config. More separate jobs in a GitHub Actions CI config

And furthermore, I want to step back and use something more modern and nicer than make, perhaps.   We should consider justfile standard.  Since these are dev tooling only, we can pick whatever we want as a dependency.  And don't necessarily just take left hook. I know that there are more modern alternatives to it, and maybe ones that are a better fit for the Python ecosystem.

turns.assistant: Acknowledged the conflation critique. Separated four concerns: (1) the list of checks; (2) the task runner (make/just/taskfile/poe/nox); (3) the hook framework (pre-commit/lefthook/prek/husky); (4) CI config (GitHub Actions). Surveyed landscape for task runners and hook frameworks. Proposed splitting P10 into P10a (task runner) + P10b (hook framework).
turns.user: use just, use lefthook.  Then continue with remaining questions.

turns.assistant: Locked task runner = just, hook framework = lefthook. Noted check-list itself is the natural consequence of all other decisions; captured in revision doc without re-asking. Asked P11 (doctor-static layout) with module-per-check importlib-loaded orchestrator as recommended.
turns.user:
I don't know about all that stuff you said, I'm not a Python programmer.

What I do know is that we should completely lean into the railway-oriented programming approach.

So whatever the executable entry points are, that should be the beginning of the railway, and any sub-modules should be invoked as part of the railway chain so that the error handling tracks are all handled uniformly by ROP.

So give me your proposal again, given that requirement.

Look at the links I gave you in your original prompt at the end that give all the background on railway-oriented programming, including the original presentation.

turns.assistant: Redesigned P11 around ROP as the spine, referencing Wlaschin's F# work (fsharpforfunandprofit.com/rop/). Core moves: every step returns Result[Success, Failure]; composition via bind/map; @safe adapters at third-party boundaries; tee for dead-end side-effects; single supervisor at top of railway. Proposed tier-1/tier-2 split with declarative Workflow/Step/WorkflowContext layer hiding ROP from authors.
turns.user: Did you read https://github.com/thewoolleyman/tab-groups-windows-list/blob/master/adws/adw_modules/engine/README.md and the subdirs like I told you?  Note that this project is checked out locally in ~/workspace if you want to grep on local filesystem rather than web tool

turns.assistant: Apologized for answering from general ROP knowledge. Read ~/workspace/tab-groups-windows-list/adws/adw_modules/engine/ README.md, __init__.py, types.py, combinators.py, executor.py. Extracted patterns: two-tier layering (public Tier 1 = Step/Workflow/WorkflowContext dataclasses; internal Tier 2 = IOResult-using executor); IOResult (not Result) for IO-tagged values; workflow-as-data with string-keyed step registry; combinators build Workflows without executing; accumulating-error executor with always_run / condition / max_attempts / data flow; public API raises while internal composes with IOResult. Proposed revised P11 with engine/ directory and two-tier split following the reference project.
turns.user:
That repo was just an example of ROP _USAGE_, not to be taken verbatim as architecture.  It specifically was designed to allow flexible ADWs to be added without ROP complexity.  Essentially a DSL.

This project is different - it has defined requirements, and no need to support authors writing high level "DSL" with no knowledge of ROP.

THerefore, that Split is unnecessary complexity.

We can just have ROP all the way down with sub-chains as necessary.

turns.assistant: Acknowledged correction. Dropped the two-tier split. Revised P11 to 'ROP all the way down' — every public function returns Result (pure) or IOResult (effectful); flow/bind/.map/.lash called directly throughout; no engine/ tier. AST checks: no raise outside io/ and errors.py; sys.exit only in __main__.py; public APIs always Result/IOResult typed; pure modules don't import impure.
turns.user: Wait a minute. How can a Python executable invoke the LLM checks? It has no idea how to invoke an LLM. The LLM-driven checks have to live at the skill level, right? The executable only handles the static checks. Am I missing something?

turns.assistant: Confirmed the correction. Python scripts handle only deterministic work; LLM-driven logic lives at the skill-markdown layer (SKILL.md, commands/<cmd>.md, template prompts). Renamed bin/doctor → bin/doctor-static (explicit about static-phase-only scope). Per-sub-command: Python script for deterministic bits + LLM follows commands/<cmd>.md for the rest. Generalized to: the LLM invokes Python scripts via the bash tool; scripts never invoke the LLM.
turns.user: Is there really any benefit to having a single main entry point as opposed to having multiple entry points for different commands in the doctor script? That corresponds to the executables we used to have.?  Because we can still have shared library code, there's no reason to couple everything into a single monolithic executable, is there? Things like that will make things harder to understand.

turns.assistant: Acknowledged conflation between ROP's 'single supervisor per process' and 'one executable for the whole plugin.' Revised P11 to multiple entry points, each a module + executable, orchestrator imports in-process, supervisors are per-process (inside if __name__ == __main__ blocks). Six executables: seed, propose-change, critique, revise, doctor, prune-history.
turns.user: You are still doing unnecessary coupling.  There's no reason that the top-level module has to be an executable.  You can have the executor be a single line shebang that specifies that it is run via Python3 (I assume this executable name is standard across all distributions and OSs.)  And the single line will just invoke the main entry point for that module.  Which should be named main.py.  Then it is directly testable as a regular module, and the unit test doesn't have to be concerned with it being an executable. The executable concern is decoupled.  And the executable can still have 100% coverage by mocking the call.

turns.assistant: Dropped if __name__ == __main__ pattern. Pure modules + thin shebang wrappers: each module exports run() and main() as ordinary functions; each wrapper is a separate file (≤5 lines: #!/usr/bin/env python3 + import + raise SystemExit(main())). Confirmed python3 is the standard portable shebang across Linux+macOS. Added AST checks: check-no-main-guard, check-supervisor-discipline (sys.exit only in bin/*), check-wrapper-shape. Proposed six wrappers under bin/ + modules under livespec/commands/ and livespec/doctor/.
turns.user:
1. What is the "top level CLI"?  I thought we got rid of the need for that. What does that executable even do?
2. What happened to vendoring livejsonparser or whatever we decided.  Did you lose that decision?
3. Why do the individual docker checks need to be executables?  Why isn't there just a single doctor-static executable that loads a single ROP chain to handle all the checks?  This makes sense because the checking logic and short-circuiting are programmatically represented via an ROP chain.  right?

turns.assistant: Addressed all three: (1) dropped bin/livespec top-level dispatcher — v005's scripts/dispatch was bash-era plumbing; each sub-command wrapper is invoked directly by SKILL.md by path; (2) re-confirmed vendoring: dry-python/returns + fastjsonschema + structlog under scripts/_vendor/, updated feedback memory to list the three locked-in vendored libs; (3) dropped per-check executables — single bin/doctor-static runs the static phase via one ROP chain (Fold.collect); per-check logic is modules only, not executables. Revised tree: six bin/*.py wrappers, _vendor/ with three libs, livespec/ package with doctor/run_static.py importing per-check modules.
turns.user: I also want a CLAUDE.md at every level of the scripts/ tree hierarchy, explaining to the LLM what it needs to be successful when working at that level.

turns.assistant: Added P22 (per-directory CLAUDE.md coverage) to the plan. Listed 11 CLAUDE.md files under scripts/ (top-level, bin/, _vendor/, livespec/, livespec/commands/, livespec/doctor/, livespec/doctor/static/, livespec/io/, livespec/parse/, livespec/validate/, livespec/schemas/). Analogous tree for tests/. Added check-claude-md-coverage target.
turns.user: confirmed.

turns.assistant: Asked P13 (purity rule) with structural dir-based recommended (parse/ and validate/ are pure, AST-checked; io/ impure; stronger than v005's prose+directive).
turns.user: [P13 answered: structural purity dir-based, AST-enforced.]

turns.assistant: Asked P14 (debug/verbose/logging): stdlib logging + LIVESPEC_LOG_LEVEL env + -v/-vv CLI flags recommended.
turns.user: Doesn't emitting JSON format logging give us the best chance of the LLM being successful and deterministic when interacting with the executables?  If so, then we should also ensure that they follow modern best practices for logging and observability.

turns.assistant: Confirmed JSON is the right call. Surveyed modern observability best practices (structured fields, run_id correlation, stderr for diagnostics, OTel compatibility, kwargs over f-strings) and Python tool landscape (structlog, stdlib+python-json-logger, loguru, OTel SDK). Revised recommendation: structlog vendored (BSD-2/MIT dual, pure Python, ~2k LOC, actively maintained, pyright-clean, ecosystem-standard). Updated feedback memory to list structlog as the third vendored lib. Re-asked P14.
turns.user: Why do we need the environment variable? If it's just for human debugging, can't you just pipe it to JQ?

turns.assistant: Correct — over-engineered. Dropped LIVESPEC_LOG_FORMAT env var and the console renderer entirely. JSON-only output to stderr, always. Kept LIVESPEC_LOG_LEVEL + -v/-vv because level filtering happens before emission (can't recover by piping). Humans pipe stderr to jq for visualization. Locked P14.
turns.user: [P14 answered: structlog vendored, JSON-only to stderr, run_id correlation, kwargs-only, LIVESPEC_LOG_LEVEL + -v/-vv for level control.]

turns.assistant: Asked P15 (exit codes): preserve v005 contract (0/1/2/3/126/127) via LivespecError hierarchy with exit_code class attribute; supervisor reads err.exit_code; no scattered sys.exit in library code.
turns.user: [P15 answered: exception hierarchy with exit_code; supervisor reads err.exit_code.]

turns.assistant: Asked P16 (file naming): shebang wrappers no extension hyphenated + modules .py snake_case as recommended (matching v005 exec convention).
turns.user: 2. The only consumers of these are the skills. We want a standard file name extension so they render properly if they are ever viewed in an IDE and also for any other tool which uses the extension for semantic meaning. Give them a standard Python file name, but still make them executable.

turns.assistant: Acknowledged .py extension. Kept hyphenated slugs to match sub-command names.
turns.user: Wait a minute, we are not done with P16.  Why would we want hyphens in it? That's not a standard Python naming convention, right? When you say "User-facing names" There's no user involved other than the skill.

turns.assistant: Correct — no human user; only the LLM following SKILL.md paths. Switched all Python filenames to snake_case + .py including bin/ wrappers. Hyphens persist only where genuine wire contract: JSON check_id values and PROPOSAL.md prose. Slug↔module mapping (hyphens↔underscores) in doctor/run_static.py's discovery loop.
turns.user: [P16 answered: snake_case + .py everywhere; bin/*.py executable.]

turns.assistant: Asked P17 (bash stub) — confirming no bash anywhere in the shipped bundle.
turns.user: [No explicit clarification — user returned to the question.]

turns.assistant: Re-asked P17 noting P20 covers dev-tooling bash separately.
turns.user: [P17 answered: no bash in shipped bundle.]

turns.assistant: Asked P18 (bash style doc fate): archive under history/v006/retired-documents/ with README recommended.
turns.user: [P18 answered: archive with README.]

turns.assistant: Asked P19 (bash-boilerplate.sh): delete both bundled file and dev-tooling symlink, no replacement.
turns.user: [P19 answered: delete both.]

turns.assistant: Asked P20 (broader bash retention): no standalone bash anywhere; all glue in just recipes / lefthook config / GitHub Actions YAML.
turns.user: [P20 answered: no standalone bash anywhere.]

turns.assistant: Interview complete. Summarized all 22 locked decisions (P1-P22) in a table. Listed vendored libs (dry-python/returns, fastjsonschema, structlog). Proposed executing remaining tasks 4-8.
turns.user:
Yes, we need to talk about the justfile  and lefthook and CI.

The justfile should be the single source of truth for invoking any dev tooling.  Any glue code to invoke Python or anything else should be encapsulated there.  And left hook and CI should just invoke justfile so we don't have duplication or leakage of any glue code.

turns.assistant: Saved the single-source-of-truth principle as feedback memory (task runner owns all invocations; hook managers and CI configs delegate). Concretized dev-tooling layer: justfile owns invocation logic; lefthook.yml only calls `just <target>`; GitHub Actions steps only `run: just <target>` with matrix parallelization for clarity. Added check-no-direct-tool-invocation enforcement. Layered ownership: .mise.toml (versions), justfile (invocations), lefthook.yml (when — pre-commit/pre-push), .github/workflows/*.yml (when — CI triggers + parallelism).
turns.user: Looks good, continue

turns.assistant: Executed remaining tasks. Wrote history/v006/proposed_changes/proposal-critique-v05-revision.md capturing per-item dispositions, governing principles (guardrails mandate, task-runner-single-source-of-truth, library-exploration-preference), and outstanding follow-ups. Wrote brainstorming/python-skill-script-style-requirements.md (~14 sections covering scope, Python version, deps, package layout, ROP, purity, type safety, linter, testing, coverage, complexity, structured logging, exit codes, file naming, dev tooling, enforcement, CLAUDE.md coverage, non-goals). Moved bash-skill-script-style-requirements.md to history/v006/retired-documents/ with a README explaining retirement. Rewrote PROPOSAL.md for v006 (bash→Python cross-references, Python skill layout with bin/ + _vendor/ + livespec/ package tree, Dependencies = python3 >=3.10 + vendored libs, Developer tooling layout = justfile + lefthook + GitHub Actions + .mise.toml + pyproject.toml + .vendor.toml + NOTICES.md, Testing approach = pytest + tmp_path + monkeypatch + pytest-cov + pytest-icdiff, doctor static-check contract preserved with Python modules, CLAUDE.md coverage DoD item added, DoD updated for Python / 100% coverage / new vendored libs / ROP orchestrator). Copied PROPOSAL.md to history/v006/PROPOSAL.md byte-identical (verified). Moved conversion plan from brainstorming/proposed_changes/ to history/v006/proposed_changes/proposal-critique-v05.md. Updated history/README.md with v006 entry after v005 (chronological order) and pointer to history/v006/PROPOSAL.md. Wrote this conversation.json.
